subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Katzenbach v. McClung (1964)
stimulus:
Beginning in 1927, the McClung family owned a restaurant that served barbecue and other foods to patrons. Their restaurant was located about eleven blocks from an interstate highway and obtained about half of its supplies from out of state. Even though the restaurant had an indoor seating capacity of over 200, it refused to seat African Americans and restricted them to take-out orders.

Congress passed the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibited discrimination in places of public accommodation. It was passed based on economic activity, given the travel of African Americans between states. However, the McClungs sued to prevent the enforcement of the Civil Rights Act. They argued that because the restaurant purchased half of its food from an in-state supplier, Congress could not regulate it because its activities were local in nature.

After a district court ruled in favor of the restaurant, the federal government appealed to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court, in a unanimous decision, held that the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was constitutional, upholding Congress' power to prohibit discrimination when it poses a significant burden to interstate commerce. The Court reasoned that because the restaurant purchased about half of its food from an out-of-state supplier and because Congress sought to resolve the problem of discrimination that was a national issue, Congress had the power to enact the legislation that regulated the restaurant.

parts.prompt: Identify the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez (1995) and Katzenbach v. McClung (1964).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Commerce Clause earns the point. The Necessary and Proper Clause or Supremacy Clause do not earn the point for this question.

scoring_points.description: Identify the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez (1995) and Katzenbach v. McClung (1964).

required_elements: Names the Commerce Clause (or Article I Commerce Clause) as the constitutional provision at issue in both cases
alternatives.correct_example: The Commerce Clause of Article I is common to both United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung.

wrong_examples: The Necessary and Proper Clause — this clause was not the central constitutional provision in either case.
The Supremacy Clause — this addresses federal preemption, not congressional regulatory power over commerce.
common_traps: Do not accept 'the interstate commerce clause' as a separate clause — the Commerce Clause is the correct name
Do not accept vague references to 'Article I' without identifying the Commerce Clause
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the constitutional clause that is common to both United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung. Acceptable identification: The Commerce Clause (Article I).

sample_responses.response: The Commerce Clause of Article I is the constitutional clause common to both United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the Commerce Clause as the constitutional basis for both cases. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The Necessary and Proper Clause is common to both cases.
sample_responses.commentary: The Necessary and Proper Clause was not the central constitutional provision in either case. Only the Commerce Clause earns the point. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung resulted in different holdings.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate information about Lopez. Second point requires explaining how the factual differences between the two cases produced different Commerce Clause outcomes — in Lopez the activity was non-economic (gun possession near schools), while in Katzenbach the activity (restaurant procurement and service) had a substantial effect on interstate commerce.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung resulted in different holdings. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case United States v. Lopez)

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from United States v. Lopez, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In United States v. Lopez, a student was arrested for carrying a firearm in a school zone in violation of the Gun-Free School Zones Act.

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from United States v. Lopez, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: The Supreme Court held in Lopez that the federal government could not regulate gun possession near schools because it was not an economic activity connected to interstate commerce.

wrong_examples: Describes only the Katzenbach facts without mentioning Lopez — this earns 0 points regardless of how accurate the Katzenbach description is.
States that Lopez involved a restaurant — confusing the two cases.
common_traps: This first point only requires accurate information about Lopez — it does not require a comparison
Do not penalize for describing the holding rather than the facts — both earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The first point is earned for accurately describing a fact or holding from the required case United States v. Lopez. Acceptable responses include: In Lopez, a student was found in possession of a firearm near a school zone, violating the Gun-Free School Zones Act. The Supreme Court held that Congress exceeded its Commerce Clause authority because gun possession near a school was not an economic activity with a substantial effect on interstate commerce.

sample_responses.response: In United States v. Lopez, a student was found to have possessed a weapon on school grounds in violation of the Gun-Free School Zones Act.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the facts of the required case United States v. Lopez. Earns the first point.

sample_responses.response: The Supreme Court held in Lopez that the federal government could not regulate gun possession near schools because it was not an economic activity connected to interstate commerce.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the holding of Lopez — the Court found the Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause power because gun possession is not an economic activity. Earns the first point.

sample_responses.response: In Lopez, the Supreme Court upheld a federal law regulating gun possession near schools as a valid use of the Commerce Clause.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the opposite of the actual holding — the Court struck down the law, not upheld it. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in United States v. Lopez and Katzenbach v. McClung resulted in different holdings. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different holdings)

required_elements: Explains the factual distinction between both cases that caused the different Commerce Clause outcomes
alternatives.correct_example: In Lopez, carrying a gun near a school was a non-economic activity with no direct connection to interstate commerce, so the Court held the Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause authority. By contrast, in Katzenbach, the restaurant's purchase of supplies from out-of-state suppliers meant its discriminatory practices had a direct effect on interstate commerce, so the Court upheld the Civil Rights Act as a valid exercise of Commerce Clause power.

wrong_examples: Describes only one case's facts — earns 1 point (b1), not 2.
Identifies the Commerce Clause is involved in both without explaining why the outcomes differed.
common_traps: Must address BOTH cases to earn the second point
The key distinction is economic vs. non-economic activity — responses that get this right in substance earn the point even with imprecise phrasing
scoring_points.official_rubric: The second point is earned for correctly explaining how the facts of both cases led to different holdings. Acceptable response: In Lopez, carrying a gun near a school was a non-economic activity with no substantial effect on interstate commerce, so the Court held the Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause authority. By contrast, the Katzenbach restaurant obtained about half its food from out-of-state suppliers, so its discriminatory practices had a direct effect on interstate commerce, and the Court upheld the Civil Rights Act as a valid Commerce Clause regulation.

sample_responses.response: In Lopez, carrying a gun near a school was a non-economic activity with no direct connection to interstate commerce, so the Court held the Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause authority. By contrast, in Katzenbach, the restaurant's purchase of supplies from out-of-state suppliers meant its discriminatory practices had a direct effect on interstate commerce, so the Court upheld the Civil Rights Act as a valid exercise of Commerce Clause power.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly explains the factual distinction between both cases — non-economic activity in Lopez vs. economic activity with interstate effects in Katzenbach — and links those facts to the different holdings. Earns the second point.

sample_responses.response: Both cases involved the Commerce Clause and had different outcomes.
sample_responses.commentary: States that the outcomes differed but does not explain why the factual differences led to those different outcomes. Earns at most the first point (b1), not the second. 0 pts for b2.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Katzenbach v. McClung reflects the democratic ideal of natural rights.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between the Katzenbach ruling and natural rights — the idea that individuals possess inherent rights not to be discriminated against that no government or business can deny. Accept any accurate explanation that ties the ruling to the natural rights principle.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Katzenbach v. McClung reflects the democratic ideal of natural rights.

required_elements: Identifies natural rights as the principle that individuals have inherent rights that cannot be taken away
Connects the Katzenbach holding to this principle by explaining how the ruling protected against racial discrimination
alternatives.correct_example: Natural rights are the inherent rights all people possess regardless of government or law. The Katzenbach decision reflects this ideal because it upheld Congress's power to prohibit racial discrimination in restaurants — affirming that African Americans have a natural right to equal access to public accommodations that no business owner can deny.

required_elements: Connects the ruling to the idea that discrimination violates natural rights and the Court's decision enforced those rights
alternatives.correct_example: The decision reflects natural rights by affirming that all people possess an inherent right to be treated equally regardless of race. By upholding the Civil Rights Act, the Court recognized that the government has an obligation to protect those rights even against private businesses.

wrong_examples: Simply stating 'everyone has rights' without explaining natural rights as a concept or connecting to the Katzenbach ruling.
Describing federalism or the Commerce Clause without addressing natural rights.
common_traps: The response must connect the ruling to natural rights specifically — not just civil rights or civil liberties generally
Accept responses that identify natural rights as inherent/pre-political rights and connect them to the anti-discrimination holding
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the decision in Katzenbach v. McClung reflects the democratic ideal of natural rights. Acceptable response: Natural rights are inherent rights that all individuals possess regardless of government action. The Katzenbach decision reflects this ideal because it upheld Congress's power to prohibit racial discrimination in restaurants, affirming that African Americans have an inherent right to equal access to public accommodations that no business can deny.

sample_responses.response: Natural rights are the inherent rights all people possess regardless of government or law. The Katzenbach decision reflects this ideal because it upheld Congress's power to prohibit racial discrimination in restaurants — affirming that African Americans have a natural right to equal access to public accommodations that no business owner can deny.
sample_responses.commentary: Defines natural rights as inherent and pre-political, then connects the Katzenbach holding to those rights by explaining how the ruling protected equal access. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The decision reflects natural rights by affirming that all people possess an inherent right to be treated equally regardless of race. By upholding the Civil Rights Act, the Court recognized that the government has an obligation to protect those rights even against private businesses.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the ruling to the concept of inherent equal rights and explains how the Court enforced those rights. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The decision upheld the Commerce Clause power of Congress to regulate interstate commerce.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the federalism/Commerce Clause rationale of the case without addressing the natural rights democratic ideal. 0 pts.
